claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you some questions about your child (or children).

**First question: How old is your child?**

The age determines which eligibility path we'll follow:
- **Under 16**: Generally automatically eligible
- **16-19**: May be eligible if in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Not eligible for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, we can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! I'd like to know if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me work through Alex's eligibility.

Since Alex is 8 years old, they fall into the "under 16" category. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is 8 years old, I would expect they are not in paid work at all, but I need to confirm this as part of the assessment process.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're only 8! They're in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This would include benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in Alex's own name (not yours). 

For an 8-year-old in primary school, this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm Alex is NOT receiving such benefits.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name. They're just a regular 8-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

Again, for an 8-year-old in primary school, this wouldn't apply, but I need to confirm Alex is NOT in an apprenticeship program.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - Alex is only 8 and in primary school, so no apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me move on to questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now about you as the claimant: Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You mentioned you live in the UK with Alex. To qualify, you need to meet at least ONE of these conditions:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common path)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working/studying/job seeking
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad

Since you mentioned you live in the UK, I assume you meet the first requirement - you normally live in the UK. Can you confirm this is the case?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - I normally live in the UK with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are you responsible for Alex?**

You've already mentioned that Alex lives with you, which means you meet the responsibility requirement. 

To be responsible for a child, you need to meet at least ONE of these:
1. **You live with the child** (which you do!)
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since Alex lives with you, you clearly meet this requirement. Shall we continue?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives with me, so I'm responsible for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member or guardian
- Anyone else

If someone else is claiming, only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child. If there's no one else claiming, or if the other person agrees to transfer the claim to you, we can proceed. If there's a dispute, HMRC would need to make the decision.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: What is Alex's current living situation?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply:

1. Is Alex currently in hospital?
2. Is Alex in local authority care?
3. Is Alex being fostered?
4. Or is Alex living normally with you at home?

Based on what you've told me (Alex lives with you), it sounds like Alex is living normally at home. Can you confirm this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex has been in hospital for a few months now. They're receiving treatment there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see. Thank you for letting me know. I hope Alex is doing okay. Let me continue with the assessment regarding Alex's hospital stay.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about Alex's hospital stay:**

You mentioned Alex has been in hospital for a few months. I need to understand the duration more precisely.

**Has Alex been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (84 days)?**

This is important because:
- Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks while a child is in hospital
- If there have been multiple hospital stays, they count cumulatively if the gaps between stays are less than 28 days

You said "a few months" - can you tell me:
1. Approximately how many weeks or months has Alex been in hospital continuously?
2. Has Alex had any breaks from hospital during this time (e.g., came home for a period)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks now - so right at that 12-week mark.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks (right at the limit), let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question: Are you regularly spending money on Alex while they are in hospital?**

This is a key factor because even if Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks (or more), you can still qualify for Child Benefit if you are regularly spending money on Alex.

This could include:
- Buying them clothes, toys, or personal items
- Providing pocket money
- Paying for things they need
- Any regular financial contribution towards Alex's needs

Are you regularly spending money on Alex while they're in hospital?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy their clothes, magazines, and favorite food to bring to them at the hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - it's clear you're still actively supporting Alex financially. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local council covering the costs of Alex's care while they're in hospital?

This would be unusual for hospital stays, but I need to confirm that the local council is NOT paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the local council isn't paying for anything - it's just the NHS hospital providing the medical treatment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Based on all the information you've provided, let me give you the final assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE** ✓

You meet all the requirements for Child Benefit for Alex. Here's why:

### Summary for Alex:
- ✓ Alex is 8 years old (under 16)
- ✓ Not in paid work
- ✓ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✓ You normally live in the UK
- ✓ You are responsible for Alex (living together)
- ✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- ✓ **Important**: Even though Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks, you continue to regularly spend money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and food), which means you still qualify for Child Benefit
- ✓ The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim)
5. **Payments**: Made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### How to Apply:
Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

I wish Alex a speedy recovery! If you have any other questions about the application process, feel free to ask.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - meeting all child-related requirements. The claimant normally lives in the UK with Alex, is responsible for Alex (living with them), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and food), which means they continue to qualify even at the 12-week threshold. The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all UK Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old child Alex, including the special circumstances around Alex's hospital stay, as they continue to regularly spend money on Alex's needs.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - meeting all child-related requirements. The claimant normally lives in the UK with Alex, is responsible for Alex (living with them), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and food), which means they continue to qualify even at the 12-week threshold. The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all UK Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old child Alex, including the special circumstances around Alex's hospital stay, as they continue to regularly spend money on Alex's needs.
